items.passage: Giant tube worms flourish around scalding hydrothermal vents on the deep ocean floor, far from any sunlight. These strange animals have no mouth, gut, or stomach of their own. Instead, billions of tiny bacteria live inside their bodies and produce food for them. The bacteria turn chemicals from the hot vents into usable energy through a special process. Because of this partnership, the worms can grow quickly in one of Earth's harshest places.

Permafrost is frozen ground that has remained solid for thousands of years across the Arctic regions. As the climate slowly warms, this ancient frozen layer is beginning to thaw. The melting soil releases powerful gases such as carbon dioxide and methane into the atmosphere. These gases trap heat and can make global warming happen even faster. Scientists across the north study the frozen ground closely to predict how much it might change.

Dreams are vivid, puzzling stories that unfold in the mind during certain stages of deep sleep. Most dreaming happens during a phase that is known as rapid eye movement sleep. During this time, the brain stays very active while the body remains still. Some researchers believe that dreams help the brain sort through memories and daily feelings. Although everyone dreams each night, many people forget most of what they saw by morning.

Mechanical clocks first appeared in medieval Europe and completely transformed how people organized their daily lives. Before their invention, most people measured time using the sun or flowing water. Early clocks used heavy weights and gears to move their hands at a steady pace. Towns often placed a large clock high in a stone tower so everyone could see it. These new machines helped workers, traders, and churches follow the same shared schedule.

Soil is a complex mixture of minerals, water, air, and decayed organic material that supports life. The tiny rock particles found in soil come from stone that has broken down over time. Rotting leaves and dead insects slowly add rich nutrients that plants need to grow. Different layers form beneath the surface, each with its own color and texture. Farmers study these layers carefully because healthy soil produces stronger and larger crops.

A black hole is a region of space where gravity pulls so strongly that nothing escapes. These strange objects form when a massive star collapses at the end of its life. Not even light can travel quite fast enough to break free from their pull. Because no light escapes, black holes cannot be seen directly through a telescope. Instead, scientists find them by carefully watching how nearby stars and hot gas behave.

Octopuses are remarkably intelligent creatures that can solve puzzles and remember the humans who feed them. Each of their eight flexible arms can move and taste on its own. In laboratories, these curious animals have learned to open jars and escape from tanks. They can also change the color of their skin to hide from hungry predators. Such clever behavior often surprises scientists because octopuses live rather short and solitary lives.

Mangrove forests grow along tropical coastlines where salty seawater meets the muddy edges of the land. Their tangled roots rise above the water and hold the soft soil in place. These sturdy roots protect the shore from strong storms and powerful ocean waves. Many fish, birds, and small crabs depend on the forest for shelter and food. Sadly, people have cleared large areas of these mangroves to build farms and cities.

Decision fatigue describes the mental tiredness that gradually builds up after making many choices in one day. As the long hours pass, the brain finds each new decision harder to face. Tired people often pick the easiest option or simply avoid choosing at all. Studies suggest that busy judges and shoppers both show this same pattern of behavior. To stay sharp, many experts make their most important decisions early in the morning.

The magnetic compass was a remarkable invention that changed the way sailors explored the open oceans. Ancient Chinese travelers first noticed that a magnetized iron needle always points north. Sailors placed the needle on a card marked with directions inside a small box. With this simple tool, ships could hold a steady course even under cloudy skies. The compass allowed brave explorers to sail far from land without becoming completely lost.

Sand dunes are shifting hills of loose sand that the wind constantly builds and reshapes over time. They form wherever there is plenty of dry loose sand and steady moving air. As wind pushes grains up the gentle slope, they pile near the top. The grains then tumble down the steeper side, so the dune slowly moves forward. Deserts and windy coastlines are the most common places to find these shifting features.

Meteor showers are dazzling displays of light that appear when Earth passes through a trail of dust. This dusty trail is usually left behind by an ancient comet passing nearby. As the tiny particles strike the air, they quickly burn up and glow brightly. People on the ground see these streaks as bright shooting stars in the sky. Some showers return each year, so patient watchers know exactly when to look up.

Spider silk is an extraordinary natural material that is both remarkably light and yet surprisingly strong. Spiders produce the silk as a liquid inside special glands within their bodies. When the liquid is pulled into the air, it hardens into a fine thread. Some silk is stronger than steel of the very same weight, which truly amazes engineers. Scientists now hope to copy this material to make ropes, nets, and medical stitches.

Air pollution has become a serious health problem in many crowded cities around the modern world. Cars, factories, and large power plants release harmful gases into the air every day. These tiny particles can damage the lungs of people who breathe them in. On still days, a thick brown haze often hangs low over busy streets. Many governments now encourage clean energy and public transport in order to reduce this pollution.

Water is a truly unusual substance whose remarkable properties together make all life on Earth possible. Unlike most other liquids, water expands and becomes lighter when it freezes into ice. That is why ice floats on the surface of a cold winter pond. Water can also dissolve many different substances, so it carries nutrients through living bodies. These rare qualities allow plants, animals, and people to survive in countless different places.

Static electricity is the familiar crackle that builds up whenever two different surfaces rub firmly together. Rubbing quickly moves tiny charged particles from one object onto the other. One surface gains an extra charge, while the other is left with less. When the stored charge finally jumps back, it makes a small spark or shock. This common effect explains why hair sometimes stands up after you pull off a wool sweater.

The invention of pottery allowed ancient people to store, cook, and carry their food far more easily. Potters shaped the soft clay by hand and then baked it in a very hot fire. The intense heat turned the fragile clay into a hard, waterproof container. These sturdy clay pots let families keep grain safe from insects and damp weather. Broken pieces of old pottery now help archaeologists understand how early cultures once lived.

The development of paper was an important advance that made writing cheaper and far more widely available. Long ago, most people wrote on stone, animal skins, or heavy clay tablets. Chinese inventors learned to soak plant fibers and press them into thin sheets. These light, cheap sheets were easy to fold, store, and send across great distances. As paper slowly spread, more people could read, record ideas, and share useful knowledge.

Stained glass windows are colorful artworks that once decorated the tall stone walls of many medieval cathedrals. Artists cut small pieces of colored glass and joined them with strips of lead. Bright sunlight passing through the glass filled the whole church with glowing patterns of color. Many windows told stories from history for the many people who could not read. Making these delicate windows required great patience, skill, and years of careful training.

Hurricanes are enormous rotating storms that gather tremendous energy over the warm waters of tropical oceans. Warm, moist air rises quickly from the sea surface and forms towering clouds. As the storm slowly grows, powerful winds begin to spin around a calm center. When a strong hurricane reaches land, it can bring heavy rain and dangerous floods. Forecasters now track these storms closely so that families have time to reach safety.
